title: El Senor Presidente
description: A dictatorship novel about fear, arbitrary power, and the texture of political terror.
descriptionParagraphs: A dictatorship novel about fear, arbitrary power, and the texture of political terror.

El Senor Presidente is usually read through its treatment of dictatorship, state terror, and Latin America. As a novel, it turns those concerns into conflicts of character, voice, setting, and social pressure rather than leaving them as abstract ideas.

Part of the work's durability lies in the way its form intensifies its themes. Readers return to it not only for subject matter but for the distinctive voice, structure, and atmosphere through which it makes dictatorship, state terror, and Latin America feel immediate.

cover.alt: El Senor Presidente cover thumbnail from Wikipedia

overview.englishDescription: A dictatorship novel about fear, arbitrary power, and the texture of political terror.
banningParagraphs: El Senor Presidente entered censorship debates as a novel associated with dictatorship, state terror, and Latin America. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around anti dictatorship and political dissent.

The earliest event currently captured here is mid-20th century in Guatemala, where Government under Jorge Ubico and successors banned circulation. The novel's portrait of authoritarianism made it difficult to circulate safely in Guatemala. It stands near the origin of the Latin American dictator-novel tradition.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: The Origins of Totalitarianism
counterReadings.author: Hannah Arendt
counterReadings.note: A foundational analysis of state terror, propaganda, and ideological conformity.

counterReadings.title: On Tyranny
counterReadings.author: Timothy Snyder
counterReadings.note: A short modern guide to resisting authoritarian politics and controlled public discourse.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
